titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Mary Hoyt Wiborg Papers
titlestmt.author: by Brooke McManus

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Describing Archives: A Content Standard

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday June 2, 2022 at 03:48

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: In English, with some materials in French.

did.abstract: The collection consists of correspondence, writings, photographs, personal papers, printed material, and electronic files by or relating to Mary Hoyt Wiborg. Materials document her life as an expatriate in France, service as a volunteer nurse in both World Wars, and work as a playwright. Included are letters from Wiborg to her father Frank Bestow Wiborg in 1918, describing her experiences at a French military hospital during World War I, and correspondence with her sisters Sara Murphy and Olga Fish, and Sara's husband Gerald. Among the other correspondents are prominent literary, artistic, political, and military figures such as Jean Cocteau, Noel Coward, Mamie Eisenhower, Manuel de Falla, John J. Pershing, and Eleanor Roosevelt. The collection additionally contains a scrapbook of clippings, correspondence, and posters for Wiborg's play Taboo , which starred African American actor Paul Robeson, and essays and memoirs about her work as a wartime nurse. Also included are two medals awarded to Wiborg by the French government and French Red Cross for her WWI service, photographs of Wiborg, family, and friends, and papers concerning her death and estate.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 8 (computer media): Restricted fragile material. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Mrs. Stuyvesant Fish, 2017, 2020 and 2021.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Correspondence, 1914-1964, undated. II. Personal Papers, circa 1910s-circa 2017. III. Computer Media, undated.

bioghist.head: Mary Hoyt Wiborg (1888-1964)
bioghist.p: Mary Hoyt "Hoytie" Wiborg, an American playwright, arts patron, and socialite who lived in New York City and Paris, was born in 1888 in Cincinnati, Ohio to Adeline Moulton Sherman and businessman Frank Bestow Wiborg. Wiborg was a volunteer nurse during World War I and earned the French Medal of Honor for her service; during the second World War, she worked for the French Red Cross. Wiborg also authored the play Taboo , performed by a predominantly Black cast, including Paul Robeson in one of his earliest stage roles, in 1922.

During Wiborg's childhood, her father's ink manufacturing business, Ault & Wiborg Company, expanded to New York, and the family thereafter maintained homes in Cincinnati, Manhattan, and Long Island. She had two sisters, amateur artist Sara Sherman Wiborg, who married painter Gerald Murphy and joined the American expatriate community in France in the 1920s, and Olga Marie Wiborg, who married Sidney Webster Fish. In 1918, Wiborg traveled to France to join the ambulance corps. She received a commendation and medal for attending to wounded soldiers at a field hospital in Montmirail, Marne, and after World War I ended, France conferred on her an apartment in Paris in appreciation for her service. During World War II, Wiborg remained in Paris at the start of the German occupation in 1940, but departed after the United States entered the war in December 1941. She was subsequently stationed with the French Red Cross in Switzerland, Germany, and Portugal. Wiborg was named honorary corporal of the 213th Regiment of the French infantry, which referred to her as its marraine, or "godmother." Additionally, she was made a Chevalier of the Legion of Honor for her part in the French Resistance. Immediately after Paris was liberated in 1944, Wiborg returned to the city and lived there until her death on March 26, 1964.
Notable members of Mary Hoyt Wiborg's family include great-uncles General William Tecumseh Sherman and U.S. Senator John Sherman.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Mary Hoyt Wiborg Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization. Various acquisitions associated with the collection have been merged and organized as a whole.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of correspondence, writings, photographs, personal papers, printed material, and electronic files by or relating to Mary Hoyt Wiborg. Materials document her life as an expatriate in France, service as a volunteer nurse in both World Wars, and work as a playwright. Included are letters from Wiborg to her father Frank Bestow Wiborg in 1918, describing her experiences at a French military hospital during World War I, and correspondence with her sisters Sara Murphy and Olga Fish, and Sara's husband Gerald. Among the other correspondents are prominent literary, artistic, political, and military figures such as Jean Cocteau, Noel Coward, Mamie Eisenhower, Manuel de Falla, John J. Pershing, and Eleanor Roosevelt. The collection additionally contains a scrapbook of clippings, correspondence, and posters for Wiborg's play Taboo , which starred African American actor Paul Robeson, and essays and memoirs about her work as a wartime nurse. Also included are two medals awarded to Wiborg by the French government and French Red Cross for her WWI service, photographs of Wiborg, family, and friends, and papers concerning her death and estate.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Mary Hoyt Wiborg Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains Mary Hoyt Wiborg's correspondence with immediate and extended family, and letters from friends and acquaintances. The bulk of the series consists of Wiborg's letters to her father in 1918, which give detailed accounts of her day-to-day experience as a nurse at a field hospital in France during World War I and include descriptions of injuries sustained by Allied soldiers, bombings and other military activities, and the impact of the war on French citizens.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Family Correspondence, and Other Correspondence.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Correspondence can also be found in Series II. Personal Papers.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by correspondent, with unidentified correspondence filed at the end of the sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains Mary Hoyt Wiborg's correspondence with her father Frank Bestow Wiborg, sisters Olga Fish and Sara Murphy, Sara's husband Gerald Murphy, her aunt Mary "Mame" Wiborg, and other relatives. Also included is a small amount of correspondence between other family members, and a letter to Frank Wiborg from the mother of an American soldier regarding Mary Hoyt Wiborg's notification of the death of her son in World War I.

did.unittitle: Murphy, Sara, letter to Mary C. Wiborg
"Letter to be printed after war," addressed to Sherman
Letters to Sara and Gerald Murphy

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by correspondent, with unidentified correspondence filed at the end of the sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of letters and brief notes to Mary Hoyt Wiborg from friends and acquaintances including poet Jean Cocteau, playwright Noel Coward, composers Manuel de Falla and Darius Milhaud, guitarist André Segovia, actor Mary Pickford, and political figures such as Dwight and Mamie Eisenhower, and Eleanor Roosevelt. Among the other correspondents are American generals John J. Pershing and Henry T. Allen, and French physician Alexis Carrel, who served in World War I as a military surgeon. Most letters relate to social engagements, and a small amount of correspondence concerns legal and financial matters.

did.unittitle: Donahue, Lester, writing on George Gershwin [photocopy]
Henderson, Neville [also includes one letter from Wiborg]
Lord Chamberlain [invitations to palace events in London]
Pershing, John J. [also includes one letter from Wiborg]
Sert, Misia [also includes two letters from Wiborg]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains writings, photographs, ephemera, and medals awarded to Mary Hoyt Wiborg for her service as a nurse in France during World War I. Included are several drafts of Wiborg's "Report of a Voluntary War Worker in the French Societé des Services Blessés Militaires, 1939-1942" and typescripts of program notes and act eleven of Taboo , as well as a scrapbook of clippings, ephemera, and correspondence documenting theatrical runs of the play. Photographs, photographic postcards, and drawings depict Wiborg and family members such as Frank B. Wiborg and Mary "Aunt Mame" Wiborg, friends including Olga Picasso and Misia Sert, monuments, and houses. The series additionally includes Wiborg's passport, French death certificate, letters of condolence to her relatives, and legal correspondence about her funeral and terms of her will. Also present are a small amount of Frank B. Wiborg's papers, consisting of a typescript diary and certificate of appointment as an aide-de-camp to the Kentucky governor.

did.unittitle: Certificate of appointment of Frank B. Wiborg as aide-de-camp to Kentucky Governor Flem D. Sampson
Death and estate of Mary Hoyt Wiborg
Diary of Frank Bestow Wiborg, January 1-June 30, 1928 [typescript]
Invitation to presidential inauguration [removed from Taboo scrapbook]
Medaille d'honneur des affaires étrangères, certificate
Medal from the French Red Cross for World War I service

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printouts and descriptions of photographs found in this collection, as well as collections related to the Wiborgs at other libraries, and notes about Mary Hoyt Wiborg compiled by family members.

did.unittitle: "Behind the Scenes (Report of a War Worker)," 1940-1941
"Hints for a Single Lady or Gentleman about to Settle in France by X - (no name)"
"Report of a Voluntary War Worker in the French Societé des Services Blessés Militaires, 1939-1942," first version
"Report of a Voluntary War Worker in the French Societé des Services Blessés Militaires, 1939-1942," [later draft?], with photograph
Scrapbook - photograph of Margaret Wycherly [?] and child
Scrapbook - programs and other theater ephemera
Untitled French poem, translated into English
Transcribed Letters and List of Memorabilia

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of transcriptions of select Wiborg family correspondence and a list of various memorabilia in the Mary Hoyt Wiborg Papers provided by the family.

did.unittitle: Transcribed Letters and List of Memorabilia
